key_insight: A machine-learning interatomic potential reveals that Mg-related transmutation defects in SiC can suppress thermal conductivity strongly, but the impact depends sharply on defect type and local configuration.
engineering_relevance: The results help predict and mitigate irradiation-driven heat-transport degradation in SiC components for advanced nuclear and fusion environments.
method: Trained MLIP4SiC-Mg on a large DFT dataset for intrinsic defects, Mg defects, and Mg-defect complexes in 3C-SiC.
Combined Green-Kubo molecular dynamics with force-error correction to compute thermal conductivity.
Used a resistance-based framework to estimate dilute-defect thermal resistivity in large supercells.
evidence_signal: The model reproduces DFT energies, forces, phonons, and thermal conductivities, and predicts corrected pristine 3C-SiC thermal conductivity of 421 W/(mK) at 300 K, consistent with experiment.
watch_item: Defect resistance is not strictly linear with concentration, so extrapolating from isolated-defect behavior to real irradiated materials can be misleading.
paper_title: Thermal Transport in SiC with Intrinsic Defects and Mg Transmutation Products
